title: Ventricles & CSF Spaces
subtitle: Recognise the main CSF-filled spaces as they appear on axial CT images.

cards.frontPrompt: Can you identify the lateral ventricles on this CT slice?
backPoints: The lateral ventricles are paired CSF-filled spaces, with one in each cerebral hemisphere.
They are the largest ventricles within the brain.
On an axial CT scan, CSF appears darker than the surrounding brain tissue.
Their size and symmetry should be compared with previous imaging and the wider clinical picture.
cards.memoryLine: Lateral ventricles: paired CSF spaces within the cerebral hemispheres.

cards.frontPrompt: Which narrow CSF space lies in the midline?
backPoints: The third ventricle is a narrow CSF-filled space in the midline of the brain.
It lies between the right and left sides of the diencephalon.
On CT it is seen centrally, below the level of the lateral ventricles.
A change in its size can be relevant when assessing for ventricular enlargement or obstruction.
cards.memoryLine: Third ventricle: the narrow midline CSF space.

cards.frontPrompt: Can you find the fourth ventricle on this lower CT slice?
backPoints: The fourth ventricle is a CSF-filled space in the posterior fossa.
It lies between the brainstem in front and the cerebellum behind.
It appears on a lower axial CT slice than the lateral and third ventricles.
Its position helps orientate you to the brainstem and cerebellum.
cards.memoryLine: Fourth ventricle: between the brainstem and cerebellum.

cards.frontPrompt: Where can CSF be seen surrounding the brainstem?
backPoints: CSF is not confined to the ventricles; it also surrounds the brain and spinal cord.
At the base of the brain, CSF-filled spaces surround the brainstem.
These spaces are collectively known as the basal cisterns.
Recognising them on CT becomes important when looking for blood around the base of the brain, such as in subarachnoid haemorrhage.
cards.memoryLine: CSF also surrounds the brainstem within the basal cisterns.

quiz.question: Which ventricles are paired, with one in each cerebral hemisphere?
Which ventricle is a narrow CSF-filled space in the midline of the brain?
Where is the fourth ventricle located?
options: Around the outside of the skull

quiz.question: How does normal CSF usually appear on a non-contrast CT scan?
Which structure is normally seen on the lowest of these CT levels?
options: Frontal horns of the lateral ventricles

quiz.question: CSF-filled spaces surrounding the base of the brain and brainstem are called:
Which statement about CSF is correct?
options: It is found only inside the lateral ventricles
It fills the ventricles and also surrounds the brain and spinal cord
It is normally visible as bright white material on CT
It is contained only within brain tissue

quiz.question: Why is recognising the CSF spaces around the brainstem clinically useful?
options: They are a common place to look for subarachnoid blood
They show the thickness of the skull
